medications.indication_rationale: Aggressive lubrication protects the exposed cornea and relieves surface pain; the mainstay of exposure keratopathy management.

medications.drug_name: topical anaesthetic drops for home use
medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetics impair corneal epithelial healing and mask worsening; must not be prescribed for home use.
No evidence of microbial keratitis; empirical antibiotics are decided after slit-lamp assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a generous ribbon of lubricating ointment, gently tape/cover the eye closed overnight, avoid the fan/draft, and use lubricant regularly (not just when painful).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Protects the exposed cornea and prevents overnight drying.

patient_education_points.point: This is very likely severe dryness of the eye surface (exposure keratopathy) because your thyroid condition holds the lids a little open and the fan dried the eye after you ran out of drops — like severely chapped lips on the eye, not necessarily a scratch or infection.
Use plenty of lubricating ointment, gently tape the eye closed overnight, and keep the fan off your face. Don't use numbing drops — they slow healing.

Because a painful light-sensitive eye needs to be examined with a slit-lamp to rule out an ulcer, see an optometrist or eye clinic today or first thing tomorrow. Go to ED immediately if your vision drops, you see a white spot on the eye, thick yellow pus appears, or you get deep pain with haloes and nausea.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Slit-lamp/fluorescein to confirm epitheliopathy and exclude ulcer/keratitis; optimise lubrication and thyroid eye disease management.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Vision loss, corneal white spot, purulent discharge, or deep pain with haloes/nausea — ED now.

must_include_items: Screen for and comment on ulcer/keratitis and angle-closure before attributing to exposure keratopathy
Recommend copious lubrication and surface protection (tape/cover, avoid fan)
Explicitly avoid prescribing home topical anaesthetic
Arrange urgent same/next-day ophthalmic assessment with a vision-loss/pus safety-net
acceptable_alternatives: Same-night ED ophthalmology if severe or vision affected
errors_of_omission: No ulcer/keratitis screen or ophthalmic referral
errors_of_commission: Assuming dry eye and advising routine review for a painful photophobic eye
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: High-risk case: passing requires urgent ophthalmic assessment + lubrication + NOT prescribing home anaesthetic. Under-triaging a painful photophobic eye or prescribing anaesthetic drops are failures.
